title: Microstructure of Highly Syndiotactic “Living” Poly(propylene)s Produced from a Titanium Complex with Chelating Fluorine-Containing Phenoxyimine Ligands (an FI Catalyst)
abstract:
Microstructure of Highly Syndiotactic “Living”
Poly(propylene)s Produced from a Titanium Complex
with Chelating Fluorine-Containing Phenoxyimine
Ligands (an FI Catalyst)
Junji Saito,1 Makoto Mitani,1 Mitsuhiko Onda,2 Jun-Ichi Mohri,1 Sei-Ichi Ishii,1 Yasunori Yoshida,1 Takashi Nakano,1
Hidetsugu Tanaka,1 Tomoaki Matsugi,1 Shin-Ichi Kojoh,1 Norio Kashiwa,1 Terunori Fujita* 1
1 Mitsui Chemicals, Inc., 580-32 Nagaura, Sodegaura, Chiba 299-0265, Japan
Fax: +81-438-64-2375; E-mail: Terunori.Fujita@mitsui-chem.co.jp
2 Mitsui Chemical Analysis & Consulting Service, Inc., 580-32 Nagaura, Sodegaura, Chiba 299-0265, Japan
Introduction
Much effort has been devoted to the development of high
performance catalysts for the living polymerization of
olefins capable of producing precisely controlled poly-
mers, such as monodisperse polymers and block copoly-
mers.[1] Recent progress in the design and synthesis of
well-defined transition metal complexes for olefin poly-
merization has spurred a creation of “living” polymers
with a wide range of novel microstructures. The elucida-
tion of the corresponding polymer microstructures has
enriched the understanding of polymerization mechan-
isms (e.g., initiation, monomer insertion/stereochemis-
try), and thus is anticipated to boost the progress in the
rational design of high performance catalysts for the liv-
ing polymerization of olefin. As a result of ligand-
oriented catalyst-design research, we found previously
that a new family of group 4 transition-metal complexes
bearing two phenoxyimine chelate ligands (named FI
Catalysts) shows high catalytic activity in olefin polymer-
ization including living polymerization.[2] We reported
details on the catalytic performance[3] and, recently, these
FI Catalysts and the corresponding early transition-metal
complexes have been actively studied involving combina-
torial methods.[4] Among FI Catalysts, a titanium FI Cata-
lyst with fluorine-containing ligand promotes the living
polymerization of propylene at room temperature to pro-
duce highly syndiotactic poly(propylene) with extremely
narrow polydispersity.[3j, 3l–n] The microstructural analysis
of this poly(propylene) may give an insight into the
design of new catalysts for the living and/or syndiospeci-
fic polymerization of propylene. In this Communication,
we report the microstructure of highly syndiotactic living
poly(propylene) produced with the titanium FI Catalyst
bis[N-(3-tert-butylsalicylidene)-2,3,4,5,6-pentafluoroani-
linato]titanium(IV) dichloride, and discuss the polymeri-
zation mechanism for this highly syndiospecific living
polymerization of propylene.
Communication: Highly syndiotactic “living” poly(pro-
pylene)s were synthesized at 258C using a bis[N-(3-tert-
butylsalicylidene)-2,3,4,5,6-pentafluoroanilinato]titanium
(IV) dichloride/MAO catalyst system, and microstructures
of the polymer were analyzed by means of 13C NMR spec-
troscopy. The syndiotactic poly(propylene) contains iso-
butyl, isopentyl and propyl end groups, suggesting that
the living polymerization of propylene was initiated via
1,2-insertion, followed by 2,1-insertion as the principal
mode of polymerization. Pentad distribution analysis
revealed that the syndiospecific polymerization proceeds
under chain-end control.
Macromol. Rapid Commun. 2001, 22, No. 13

 Microstructure of Highly Syndiotactic “Living” Poly(propylene)s ...
1073
Experimental Part
All manipulations of air- and/or water-sensitive compounds
were performed under a dry nitrogen atmosphere using stan-
dard Schlenk techniques. Toluene used as the solvent for
polymerizations (Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Ltd.) was
dried over Al2O3. Propylene was obtained from Mitsui Chem-
icals Inc. Methylaluminoxane (MAO) was purchased from
Albemarle as a 1.2 m solution in toluene, and the remaining
trimethylaluminium was evaporated in vacuo prior to use.
Polymerizations of propylene were performed in a glass
reactor (500 cm3) equipped with mechanical stirrer and tem-
perature probe. Toluene (250 cm3) was introduced to the
nitrogen-purged reactor and stirred vigorously (600 rpm).
The toluene was kept at 258C, and then the propylene gas
feed (100 L/h) was started. Polymerization was initiated by
adding a 1.25 m solution of MAO in toluene and then a 0.01
m solution of complex 1 in toluene into the reactor with vig-
orous stirring. After 15 or 300 min, isobutyl alcohol (10 cm3)
was added to terminate the polymerization, and the propyl-
ene gas feed was stopped. The resulting mixture was added
to acidic methanol (1000 cm3 including 2 cm3 of conc. HCl).
The precipitated polymer was collected by filtration, washed
with methanol (26200 cm3), and dried (808C, vacuum
oven, overnight).
13C NMR spectra of poly(propylene)s were obtained using
tetrachloroethane-d2 as the solvent at 1208C using an LA-
500 spectrometer at 125 MHz. Molecular weight distribu-
tions (M
—
w/M
—
n) of the poly(propylene)s were determined on a
Waters 150-C gel permeation chromatograph equipped with
three TSKgel columns (two sets of TSKgelGMHHR-H(S)HT
and TSKgelGMH6-HTL) at 1458C using poly(propylene)
calibration. o-Dichlorobenzene was employed as the solvent
at a flow rate of 1.0 ml/min. Peak melting temperatures (Tm)
were determined by means of DSC with a Shimadzu DSC-60
differential scanning calorimeter, measured upon re-heating
the polymer sample to 2008C at a heating rate of 108C/min.
Results and Discussion
Titanium FI Catalyst 1 (Scheme 1) used in this research
was prepared by reacting TiCl4 with two equivalents of
the lithium salt of N-(3-tert-butylsalicylidene)-2,3,4,5,6-
pentafluoroaniline in diethyl ether according to a proce-
dure described previously.[3k, 3m] We already reported that
the catalyst system 1/MAO is capable of catalyzing the
living polymerization of propylene at room temperature
to produce syndiotactic poly(propylene) with extremely
narrow polydispersity.[3m] Polymerizations of propylene
were carried out at 258C under an atmospheric pressure
of propylene for 15 and 300 min to acquire low and high
M
—
n syndiotactic living poly(propylene)s. Peak melting
temperatures Tm, M
—
n and M
—
w/M
—
n values of the poly(propy-
lene)s produced are listed in Table 1. According to the
methyl triad regions in the 13C NMR spectra, it could be
determined that the polymers contain 98% (entry 1) or
87% (entry 2) rr triads. To the best of our knowledge,
98% is the highest value reported to date for the rr triads
in syndiotactic living poly(propylene)s. Figure 1 displays
the 13C NMR spectrum of the poly(propylene) with M
—
n =
2000 (entry 1). The spectrum displays peaks at 14.2 ppm,
20.6 ppm, 22.4–22.8 ppm, and 23.3–23.6 ppm in addi-
tion to three major sets of peaks derived from the main-
chain carbon atoms. The peaks at 14.2 ppm and 20.6
ppm, 22.4–22.8 ppm, and 23.3–23.6 ppm were assigned
to the methyl carbon atoms of propyl, isobutyl and iso-
pentyl groups, and isobutyl groups (end group: propyl 1.6
mol-%, isobutyl 1.5 mol-%, isopentyl 1.5 mol-%), respec-
tively,a based on a comparison with literature data.[5]
Scheme 1.
Bis[N-(3-tert-butylsalicylidene)-2,3,4,5,6-penta-
fluoroanilinato]titanium(IV) dichloride (1).
a
The peaks at 35.5 ppm, 36.5 ppm and 25.6 ppm were as-
signed to the Sab CH2 carbon atom of the isopentyl and to the
CH carbon atom of the isobutyl end group, respectively.
Figure 1.
(a) 13C NMR spectrum of syndiotactic living poly-
(propylene) (entry 1) and (b) the horizontal expansion of the
methyl region.

Poly(propylene)s Produced from a Titanium Complex
with Chelating Fluorine-Containing Phenoxyimine

2 Mitsui Chemical Analysis & Consulting Service, Inc., 580-32 Nagaura, Sodegaura, Chiba 299-0265, Japan

well-defined transition metal complexes for olefin poly-
merization has spurred a creation of “living” polymers
with a wide range of novel microstructures. The elucida-
tion of the corresponding polymer microstructures has
enriched the understanding of polymerization mechan-
isms (e.g., initiation, monomer insertion/stereochemis-
try), and thus is anticipated to boost the progress in the
rational design of high performance catalysts for the liv-
ing polymerization of olefin. As a result of ligand-
oriented catalyst-design research, we found previously

complexes have been actively studied involving combina-
torial methods.[4] Among FI Catalysts, a titanium FI Cata-

fic polymerization of propylene. In this Communication,

bis[N-(3-tert-butylsalicylidene)-2,3,4,5,6-pentafluoroani-
linato]titanium(IV) dichloride, and discuss the polymeri-
zation mechanism for this highly syndiospecific living

m solution of complex 1 in toluene into the reactor with vig-

with methanol (26200 cm3), and dried (808C, vacuum
13C NMR spectra of poly(propylene)s were obtained using
tetrachloroethane-d2 as the solvent at 1208C using an LA-
500 spectrometer at 125 MHz. Molecular weight distribu-
n) of the poly(propylene)s were determined on a
Waters 150-C gel permeation chromatograph equipped with
three TSKgel columns (two sets of TSKgelGMHHR-H(S)HT
and TSKgelGMH6-HTL) at 1458C using poly(propylene)
calibration. o-Dichlorobenzene was employed as the solvent
at a flow rate of 1.0 ml/min. Peak melting temperatures (Tm)
were determined by means of DSC with a Shimadzu DSC-60
differential scanning calorimeter, measured upon re-heating
the polymer sample to 2008C at a heating rate of 108C/min.
Titanium FI Catalyst 1 (Scheme 1) used in this research
was prepared by reacting TiCl4 with two equivalents of
the lithium salt of N-(3-tert-butylsalicylidene)-2,3,4,5,6-
pentafluoroaniline in diethyl ether according to a proce-
dure described previously.[3k, 3m] We already reported that
the catalyst system 1/MAO is capable of catalyzing the
living polymerization of propylene at room temperature
to produce syndiotactic poly(propylene) with extremely
narrow polydispersity.[3m] Polymerizations of propylene
were carried out at 258C under an atmospheric pressure
of propylene for 15 and 300 min to acquire low and high
n syndiotactic living poly(propylene)s. Peak melting
lene)s produced are listed in Table 1. According to the
methyl triad regions in the 13C NMR spectra, it could be
determined that the polymers contain 98% (entry 1) or
87% (entry 2) rr triads. To the best of our knowledge,
98% is the highest value reported to date for the rr triads
in syndiotactic living poly(propylene)s. Figure 1 displays
the 13C NMR spectrum of the poly(propylene) with M
2000 (entry 1). The spectrum displays peaks at 14.2 ppm,
20.6 ppm, 22.4–22.8 ppm, and 23.3–23.6 ppm in addi-
tion to three major sets of peaks derived from the main-
chain carbon atoms. The peaks at 14.2 ppm and 20.6
ppm, 22.4–22.8 ppm, and 23.3–23.6 ppm were assigned
to the methyl carbon atoms of propyl, isobutyl and iso-
pentyl groups, and isobutyl groups (end group: propyl 1.6
mol-%, isobutyl 1.5 mol-%, isopentyl 1.5 mol-%), respec-
tively,a based on a comparison with literature data.[5]

fluoroanilinato]titanium(IV) dichloride (1).
The peaks at 35.5 ppm, 36.5 ppm and 25.6 ppm were as-
signed to the Sab CH2 carbon atom of the isopentyl and to the
CH carbon atom of the isobutyl end group, respectively.
(a) 13C NMR spectrum of syndiotactic living poly-
(propylene) (entry 1) and (b) the horizontal expansion of the
Thus, the poly(propylene) contains propyl, isobutyl, and
isopentyl end groups, suggesting that the living polymeri-
zation was initiated exclusively by 1,2-insertion, followed
by 2,1-insertion (ca. 70%) as the principal mode of poly-
merization (Scheme 2). As far as we are aware, a predo-
minant 2,1-insertion mechanism for chain propagation is
unusual for a titanium complex.
Density functional theory (DFT) calculations per-
formed on an active species derived from complex 1 indi-
cate that the fluorine substituent adjacent to the imine-
nitrogen interacts with a b-hydrogen of a polymer chain
to fix a chain conformation near the polymerization reac-
tion center. This fixation may result in a relatively unfa-
vorable steric congestion for the 1,2-insertion of propyl-
ene.b The pentad distribution analysis of the 13C NMR
spectra of the polymers (Figure 2) indicates the presence
of single m stereodefects, rrrm and rrmr, as the predomi-
nant source of stereoirregularity in the polymer micro-
structure. This suggests that the syndiospecific living
Results of the polymerization of propylene with the catalyst system 1/MAO. Polymerization conditions: 250 cm3 toluene,
Mechanisms for initiation and chain propagation in the polymerization of propylene using the catalyst system
13C NMR spectra of the methyl pentad regions of the
poly(propylene)s derived from 1/MAO: (a) entry 1 and (b) entry
Since the corresponding non-fluorinated complex produces
practically atactic poly(propylene)s, the syndiospecific poly-
merization behavior probably originates from the interaction
between fluorine and b-hydrogen. At present, however, we
cannot give a concrete figure for propylene polymerization.
This is because the titanium complex has two unsymmetrical
bidentate ligands and, thus, the complex potentially pos-
sesses more than one (probably three) active species in
which a fluorine adjacent to the imine-nitrogen can interact
with a b-hydrogen of a polymer chain.
Microstructure of Highly Syndiotactic “Living” Poly(propylene)s ...
polymerization proceeds by a chain-end control mechan-
ism. The rr triad values achieved with the system 1/MAO
(98%, 87%) are extremely high for polymers produced
via chain-end control. The interaction between fluorine
and b-hydrogen mentioned above may be responsible for
this very high syndiospecificity. Further work regarding
DFT calculations and 13C NMR analyses of the polymers
is in progress in order to further elucidate the polymeriza-
In summary, the microstructures of highly syndiotactic
living poly(propylene)s synthesized with an FI Catalyst/
MAO system have been elucidated by means of 13C NMR
spectroscopy. An unusual polymerization mechanism
probably originating from a fixation of the polymer con-
formation near the polymerization reaction center has
been suggested for titanium catalyst-promoted polymeri-
zations of propylene. The results presented in this paper
along with our previous results may contribute to a break-
through with respect to accomplishing the living and/or
highly stereospecific polymerization of a-olefins.
Acknowledgement: We would like to thank Dr. M. Mullins for
fruitful discussions and suggestions.
